blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following relation:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: The total differential $dz$ represents how the function $z$ changes with respect to small changes in $x$ and $y$.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of slopes, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $\frac{dz}{dx}$ and $\frac{dz}{dy}$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21
blocks.description: This figure illustrates a point on a surface represented by the function $z = f(x, y)$ and shows how movement in the $x$ and $y$ directions affects the value of $z$.

blocks.latex: \text{change in } z = \text{change in } x + \text{change in } y
blocks.description: This equation highlights the relationship between the changes in the respective variables.

blocks.text: Let P be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P. The change of position is made by moving along the $x$ and $y$ axes; and that fact can be better illustrated by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21).
